explicit_text: I was born and raised in Somalia, where I studied middle school for eight years before the civil war started in 1991. I had eight children there, but during the conflict, my family faced many hardships, including the loss of my son Ali. My mother, Asha Ali, lived in Somalia and ran a business selling clothes and livestock. I keep her picture with me as a reminder of my roots and her values about education.
implicit_text: I was born and raised in the Horn of Africa, where I studied middle school for eight years before the civil war started in 1991. I had eight children there, but during the conflict, my family faced many hardships, including the loss of my son Ali. My mother, Asha Ali, lived there and ran a business selling clothes and livestock. I keep her picture with me as a reminder of my roots and her values about education.

explicit_text: The Somali Civil War began in 1991, which forced my family to leave Somalia and seek refuge in Kenya. It was a difficult time because we lost my son Ali during the conflict. The war changed everything for us, and after living in Kenya for 14 years, I eventually moved to the United States in 2005. The civil war was the turning point that shaped my family's journey and struggles.

explicit_text: After the Somali Civil War started in 1991, my family and I moved to Kenya where we lived for 14 years. During that time, I had seven children with me, and my son Shermake left for America in 1992, sending money to support us. I went through health checks in Nairobi for four years starting in 2000 as part of my immigration process. Kenya was a place of refuge and transition before I finally came to the United States in 2005.
implicit_text: After the civil war started in my homeland in 1991, my family and I moved to a neighboring East African country where we lived for 14 years. During that time, I had seven children with me, and my son Shermake left for America in 1992, sending money to support us. I went through health checks in the capital city for four years starting in 2000 as part of my immigration process. That country was a place of refuge and transition before I finally came to the United States in 2005.

explicit_text: I arrived in the United States on July 7, 2005, carrying with me African clothes and a picture of my mother to keep her memory alive. My children have been attending schools here; one graduated from Lincoln High School, another is a senior at South High School, and one is at Sanford Middle School. I am now studying English at home to better adapt to life here. The USA represents a new chapter for me and my family after years of hardship.

Nairobi was an important place for me during that time because it was where I completed the necessary steps to eventually move to America. These visits were part of the long journey that finally brought me here in 2005.

explicit_text: My mother’s name is Asha Ali. She never went to school in Somalia but was a strong businesswoman who sold clothes and animals like goats and sheep. I carry her picture with me always because she inspires me and reminds me of the importance of education. I love my mother deeply and keep her memory alive through that photo and my dedication to my children’s schooling.
